content.full_text:
Farn Porrrrcal PnecrrcEs CouvussroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
July 23,2004
Diane M. Fishburn
Olson, Hagel & Fishburn,LLP
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425
Sacramento, CA 95814-4602
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. .4.-04-110
Dear Ms. Fishburn:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Jack O'Connell, the
O'Connell2006 committee, and the O'Connell2}}2 committee for advice regarding the
campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the..Act,).r
QUESTIONS
I ' May O'Connell 2006 receive contributions for the Noverrb er 2006 general
election prior to the March 2006 primary election?
2. what methods of obtaining oecupation and employer information are
acceptable compliance of Government Code section g5700?
3. what records must the committee keep to document the collection of
occupation and employer information required by g5700?
4. What is the appropriate treatrnent of interest income earned on pre-proposition
34 funds?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Yes.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appeax at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. 4-04-110
Page No. 2
2. Section 84104 requires the committee to report accurate information.
Therefore, so long as the information reported is accuratg there is no specific
method mandated for obtaining the information required by section 85700.
3. Your question is general in nafure. we can say, generally speaking, that there
is no specific method for keeping records on the information obtained
pursuant to section 85700. Ultimately, the sufficiency of any record keeping
in a particular instance will be assessed against the requirements of regulation
18401.
4. Interest eamed on pre-34 funds that are held in a separate money market
account maybe hansferred as pre-34 funds subject to section 85306,
subdivision (c).
FACTS
Jack O'Connell was elected inZOO2 to the office of State Superintendent of
Public Instruction. o'Connellzaoz was formed to support Mr. O'Connell,s2ao}
election campaign. O'Connell2006 was formed to $ryport Mr. O'Connell's reelection
campaign in2006.
ANALYSIS
l. May O'Connell 2006 receive contributionsfor the November 2006 general election
prior to the March 2006 primary election?
Government Code section 85318 allows "a candidate for elective state office" to
accept conkibutions towards the general election prior to the primary election. As the
Superintendent of Public Instnrction is an elective state officeunder section 82024,171r.
O'Connell may raise funds into a general election account or committee prior to the
conclusion of theprimary election in20fl6. In the event Mr. O'Connell wins reelection _
in the primary, any contributions held for the general election must be returned to
contributors, less fund-raising expenses. ($ 8531S.)
2. What methods of obtaining occtpation and employer information are acceptable
compliance of Government Code section 85700?
Section 85700 requires a candidate to return contributions within 60 dap of their
receipt if the candidate does not have on file certain information in his or her records,
such as the occupation of the contributor and his or her e,rnployer. ($ 85700, subd. (a).)
Section 84211requires, among other things, that a candidate report the namq address,
occupation and ernployer of any individual who contributes $100 ormore in a calendar
year. If a candidate is unable to acquire this information, section 85700 requires the
candidate to return the contribution. In your letter, you state a committee sometimes is
unable to obtain the required information from the contributor and must instead rely on


File No. A-04-110
Page No. 3
other sources for the information. You pose nine different possible sources for
information:
o Occupation and employer information obtained from committee records of prior
contributions to O'Connell2002 or another committee controlled by Jack O'Connell;
r Personal knowledge by Jack O'Connell or O'Connell20[l6 committee staffand
consultants regarding a donor's occupation and employer information;
o Publicly available profiles of donors (e.g., company profiles on the Internet);
o Publicly available professional license databases such as Califomia State Bar
Association records or Califomia Medical Association records;
. Publicly avas.lable professional organization rosters such as the California Society of
Certified Public Accountants or the International Chiropractors Association of
Califomia;
o Occupation and ernployerinformation obtained from campaign statements filed with
the Secretary of State by other committees;
o Telephone follow-up with employees, secretaries, or assistants ofthe donor;
o Requesting donor information from the host of a fund-raising event; and
o Personal knowledge by third parties such as a fund raiser or otherpersons regarding a
donor's occupation and ernployer.
Section 85700 does not require that the information is obtained firsthand from the
contributor. Section 85700, subdivision (a), requires that a candidate or committee have
the required information on file at the time of receiving the contribution or within 60 days
thereafter. Therefore, so long as accurate information required to be disclosd under
section 84211is on file at the time of receiving the contribution or within 60 days
thereafter, no particular method for obtaining the information is required by section
85700.
3. What records must the committee keep to document the collection of occupation and
employer information?
Under section 85700, the Act requires a committee to return a contribution within
60 days if the committee is unable to obtain thedonor's occupation and employer on file
in the records of the committee. So that compliance with the Act's 60-day rule may be
established in the event of audit, the Commission adopted regulation 18570, subdivision
(d), requiring the committee to maintain in its records the date the occupation and
employer information is obtained.
Section 84lU states that it is the duty of each candidate and treasurer to maintain
the records necessary to complete the reports required to be filed under the Act.
Regulation 18401, which further specifies the records that must be maintained by
committees, does not specifr the form of records kept by the committee as they relate to
occupation and employer information. You ask whether any of various potential forms of
record keeping are acceptable:


FileNo. 4-04-110
Page No. 4
Hand-written or tlped notations kept in the committee's files to preserve
information which is obtained orally from committee staff or third pmties;
Direct input of occupation and employer information into carnpaign-reporting
software (the information is stored electronically and can be printed and viewed
for audit purposes);
Printing documentation obtained from publicly available databases or directories
and placing theprint-out in committee files; and
Presenring correspondence and other documentation from committee staffor third
parties regarding occupation and ernployer inforrnation.
Generally speaking the methods you describe above are sufficient methods for
keeping records of the information obtained pursuant to section 85700. IJltimately, the
sufficiency of any record keeping^in a particular instance will be assessed against the
requirements of regulation 18401.2 For instancg with respect to electronic contributor
records, we have advised that they may be maintained on a CDROM format.
(McAndrews'Advice Letter, No. ,{-03-197.) Thatrecord keeping is a sepmate obligation
of candidates and treasurers. Having otherwise sufficient documentation of a fact does
not excuse a candidate or treasurer from disclosing true and correct information.
4. What is the appropiate treatment of interest income earned on pre-Proposition 34
funds?
You indicate that O'Connell 2002 possesses contibutions which were received
prior to November 6,2002, the effective date ofmost Proposition 34 provisions
applicable to candidates for statewide office. These funds earn interest in a separate
money market account. As these funds may be transferred without attribution pursuant to
section 85306, subdivision (c), you ask whether the interest earned may also be
consideredpart ofthe pre-34 funds forpurposes of section 85306.
The Commission has advised that committees report interest on disclosure forms
as a miscellaneous increase to cash. (Trask-Lee Advice Letter, No. I-04-047; Odom
Advice Letter, No. I-91-172.) This system regards the committee's contributions on hand
and the interest earned by those contributions as an undifferentiated pot of money. As a
result, interest earned by O'Connell2}O21naybe transfbrred as part of the pre-34 funds
pursuant to section 85306, subdivision (c).r
2 See also the Sutton Advice Letter, No. A-9G287, discussing the pertinent record-keeping rules
in the context of de minimis contributions.
3 We note that regulation lSi24,subdivision (b), allows funds to be deposited into a money
market account, but requires the funds be hansferred back into the campaign bank account prior to
expenditure:
"(b) The candidate may transfer funds from the campaigrr bank account to certificates of deposit,
interest-bearing savings accounts, money market accounts, or similar accounts which shall be established
only for funds for the same elective office for which the campaip bank account was established. Prior to
expenditure, the funds shall be re-deposited in the candidate's campaign bank account."
a
a
a


File No. A-04-110
Page No. 5
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
CSTjg
I:\Advicelks\04-l 10
SincerelS
Luisa Menchaca
a
1,oa1^
85 LY
By: C. Scott Tocher
Counsel, Legal Dvision
